text.title: PMBOK 7 Six Core Principles
text.summary: The six PMBOK 7 guiding principles with application questions and common violation patterns for each.

rule.statement: Run the six-principle audit before committing to any non-trivial PM decision; auditing after execution recovers nothing.
rule.rationale: Principles function as pre-commitment devices; they catch violations at the cheapest moment (before execution) rather than at the most expensive (after delivery).

section.title: 1 — Adopt Holistic View
section.p: See the project as part of a larger system: organization, portfolio, market, and ecosystem. Decisions that optimize locally often suboptimize the whole.

list.item: Application question: "Does this decision consider upstream and downstream effects outside our project boundary?"
Violation pattern: shipping a feature that solves this project's problem while creating integration debt for three adjacent systems

section.title: 2 — Focus on Value
section.p: Deliver outcomes (user or business results), not just outputs (artifacts or features). Measure value realization explicitly, not by assumption.

list.item: Application question: "Can we measure the value this delivers, and to whom?"
Violation pattern: 100% delivery rate against scope with 30% outcomes realized — "Focus on Value" is not honored without measurable success criteria

rule.statement: Define at least one project-specific KPI for "value" before starting execution (e.g., NPS+activation, escaped defects, revenue uplift).
rule.rationale: "Focus on Value" without a measurement contract becomes virtue signaling that no one can act on.

section.title: 3 — Embed Quality
section.p: Quality is built in from the start, not tested in at the end. Covers both product quality (defect rate, performance) and process quality (review rigor, observability).

list.item: Application question: "Where is quality verified in the workflow — and is it before or after cost is committed?"
Violation pattern: equating "Embed Quality" with "add tests" while skipping code review, observability, and runbooks

section.title: 4 — Lead Accountably
section.p: Ethical, transparent leadership with clear decision rights. Accountability requires naming who decides, not just who recommends.

list.item: Application question: "Is there a named person accountable for this decision, and do they have the authority to make it?"
Violation pattern: committee decisions with no single accountable — everyone agrees in the room, no one owns the outcome

section.title: 5 — Integrate Sustainability
section.p: Consider environmental, social, and economic impact. Requires concrete metrics; "we care about sustainability" without numbers is insufficient.

list.item: Application question: "What is the measurable environmental or social impact of this deliverable, and is it acceptable?"
Violation pattern: skipping the sustainability question entirely in tech projects because it feels irrelevant — cloud carbon, data retention, accessibility all count

section.title: 6 — Build Empowered Teams
section.p: Trust, autonomy, and collaboration. Empowerment without explicit decision rights is hollow — teams need to know what they can decide without escalation.

list.item: Application question: "What decisions can this team make autonomously today, and is that boundary documented?"
Violation pattern: claiming empowerment while requiring PM sign-off on every PR merge or minor scope decision

rule.statement: When two principles conflict, weight them by project type — a regulated medical product weights Embed Quality 5/5; an internal hackathon weights Focus on Value 5/5 — and document the weighting in constitution.md.
rule.rationale: The six principles have no built-in priority order; without project-level weighting, the most vocal stakeholder's preferred principle wins by default.

text.reference: Emits a six-row PASS/WARN/FAIL markdown audit table for a decision
